perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees to obtain and maintain, during the Term and for five (5) years after the Term, commercial general liability insurance, including products liability insurance, with minimum "A-" AM Best rated insurance carriers, in each case with limits of not less than five million dollars ($5,000,000) per occurrence and in the aggregate. All deductibles/retentions will be the responsibility of the named insured. Pfizer and its Affiliates will be an additional insured on Exact's commercial general liability and products liability policies, and be provided with a waiver of subrogation. To the extent of its culpability, all coverages of Exact will be primary and non-contributing with any similar insurance carried by Pfizer.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees to obtain and maintain, during the Term, commercial general liability insurance, including products liability insurance. All deductibles/retentions will be the responsibility of the named insured.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the minimum insurance rating, the specific dollar amount, the additional insured clause, the waiver of subrogation and the primary coverage clause, this modification introduces legal risk. Insurance requirements protect each party from liability and ensure adequate coverage. Removing the specific AM Best rating and minimum coverage weakens the protection, creating a legal contradiction with standard business practices and potentially violating implied duties of good faith and fair dealing. The applicable jurisdiction is New York, as per section 9.10.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The omission of specific insurance requirements can be viewed as a violation of the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under New York law, as it undermines the reasonable expectations of the parties regarding financial protection in case of liabilities. The original clause ensures there is an insurance safety net, while the modified version opens the door to inadequate or sham insurance. Note: While § 1-304 specifically references the UCC, the duty of good faith is broadly applicable to all contracts in New York.

Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes essential insurance requirements, contradicting the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC § 1-304, states that every contract imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. By removing the specific insurance rating, minimum coverage amount, and additional insured clauses, the modified text undermines the reasonable expectation of financial protection, thus contradicting the law.

perturbation.original_text: In the event any payments made pursuant to this Agreement become subject to withholding taxes under the laws or regulation of any jurisdiction, the Party making such payment shall deduct and withhold the amount of such taxes for the account of the payee to the extent required by Applicable Law and such amounts payable to the payee shall be reduced by the amount of taxes deducted and withheld. Any such withholding taxes required under Applicable Law to be paid or withheld shall be an expense of, and borne solely by, the payee. To the extent that the Party making a payment is required to deduct and withhold taxes on any payments under this Agreement, the Party making such payment shall pay the amounts of such taxes to the proper Governmental Authority in a timely manner and promptly transmit to the payee an official tax certificate or other evidence of such withholding sufficient to enable the payee to claim such payments of taxes. The payee shall provide any tax forms to the Party making such payment that may be reasonably necessary in order for such Party not to withhold tax or to withhold tax at a reduced rate under an applicable bilateral income tax treaty. The payee shall use commercially reasonable efforts to provide any such tax forms to the Party making the payment at least thirty (30) days prior to the due date for any payments for which the payee desires that the Party making the payment apply a reduced withholding rate. Each Party shall provide the other with reasonable assistance to enable the recovery, as permitted by Law, of withholding taxes, VAT, or similar obligations resulting from payments made under this Agreement, such recovery to be for the benefit of the Party bearing such withholding tax or VAT.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event any payments made pursuant to this Agreement become subject to withholding taxes under the laws or regulation of any jurisdiction, the Party making such payment shall deduct and withhold the amount of such taxes. Any such withholding taxes required under Applicable Law to be paid or withheld shall be an expense of, and borne solely by, the payee.
perturbation.explanation: The original text describes how the paying party is required to provide documentation and pay those taxes to the governmental authority. The modified text removes the paying party's obligation to provide tax certificates or evidence of withholding to the payee, as well as assistance in recovering withholding taxes. This could violate IRS regulations that require payers to provide documentation (like Form 1042-S) to payees for withheld taxes, particularly for international payments. This creates a contradiction with federal tax law regarding withholding responsibilities.
perturbation.contradicted_law: IRS regulations regarding withholding tax documentation
perturbation.law_citation: 26 U.S. Code § 1461 - Return and payment of withheld tax
perturbation.law_explanation: 26 U.S. Code § 1461 requires every person required to deduct and withhold any tax to make a return and payment of such tax to the IRS. Implicit in this requirement is the need to provide documentation to the payee, allowing them to properly account for the withheld taxes. The modified text's omission of the payer's responsibility to provide documentation contradicts this requirement.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 26 / Subtitle A / CHAPTER 3 / Subchapter B / § 1461 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 26 USC 1461 : Liability for withheld tax Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 26-INTERNAL REVENUE CODE Subtitle A-Income Taxes CHAPTER 3-WITHHOLDING OF TAX ON NONRESIDENT ALIENS AND FOREIGN CORPORATIONS Subchapter B-Application of Withholding Provisions Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments Effective Date §1461. Liability for withheld tax Every person required to deduct and withhold any tax under this chapter is hereby made liable for such tax and is hereby indemnified against the claims and demands of any person for the amount of any payments made in accordance with the provisions of this chapter. ( Aug. 16, 1954, ch. 736, 68A Sta
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the original text's obligation for the paying party to provide tax certificates or evidence of withholding to the payee and assistance in recovering withholding taxes. 26 U.S. Code § 1461 requires every person required to deduct and withhold any tax to make a return and payment of such tax to the IRS. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not directly mention the need to provide documentation to the payee, the Law Explanation argues that this is implicit in the requirement to make a return and payment of taxes, and the Perturbation Explanation bolsters this. Thus, omitting the clause requiring the payer to provide documentation directly impacts the payee's ability to properly account for the withheld taxes, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Exact shall use commercially reasonable efforts to provide six (6) month notice prior to the expiry of the Term, or in the case of termination by Pfizer under Section 8.4, within the applicable notice period in advance of the effective date of such termination, that Exact intends for Pfizer to continue providing Advertising services for the Product pursuant to Section 3.2(d).
perturbation.changed_text: Exact may, at its discretion, notify Pfizer if it intends for Pfizer to continue providing Advertising services for the Product pursuant to Section 3.2(d).
perturbation.explanation: The original text describes that Exact must 'use commercially reasonable efforts to provide six (6) month notice' before the expiration of the term to allow the other party to continue providing advertising services for the product. The modified text states that Exact 'may, at its discretion, notify Pfizer'. By changing the wording from 'shall use commercially reasonable efforts' to 'may, at its discretion,' a significant obligation is removed, creating legal risk for Pfizer. This violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, particularly given the significant investment by Pfizer in marketing and promotion.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The omission of the requirement to use commercially reasonable efforts to provide notice impacts Pfizer's ability to plan and potentially benefit from its marketing investments during the tail period. The change of words from 'shall use commercially reasonable efforts' to 'may, at its discretion,' is a contradiction to the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under New York law, as it undermines the reasonable expectations of the parties.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates 'commercially reasonable efforts' for notice, while the changed text makes notification discretionary. This weakens Pfizer's position and potentially contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, as the reasonable expectation of a notice period is undermined by the removal of the mandatory language. Since the scraped law snippets are missing, the conclusion is based on the perturbation and law explanation.
